section.title: The rule d^2=0

subsection.title: Why second exterior derivatives vanish
subsection.p:
Start with a function. Let f(x,y,z)=x^2z+xy. Then df=(2xz+y)\,dx+x\,dy+x^2\,dz. Now take d again:
d(df) \amp= d(2xz+y)\wedge dx + d(x)\wedge dy + d(x^2)\wedge dz.
Compute the coefficient differentials: d(2xz+y)=2z\,dx+dy+2x\,dz, d(x)=dx, \qquad d(x^2)=2x\,dx. So
d(df) \amp= (2z\,dx+dy+2x\,dz)\wedge dx + dx\wedge dy + 2x\,dx\wedge dz.
The repeated wedge dx\wedge dx vanishes:
d(df) \amp= dy\wedge dx+2x\,dz\wedge dx + dx\wedge dy + 2x\,dx\wedge dz.
Now pair the terms: dy\wedge dx=-dx\wedge dy, and dz\wedge dx=-dx\wedge dz. Thus
d(df) \amp= -dx\wedge dy-2x\,dx\wedge dz + dx\wedge dy + 2x\,dx\wedge dz \amp= 0.
The cancellation came from two facts: dx\wedge dx=0 and dx\wedge dy=-dy\wedge dx. The wedge product supplies the signs that make the second derivative cancel.
The same thing happens to every sufficiently smooth function.

statement.p: Let f(x,y,z) have continuous second partial derivatives on an open region. Then \boxed{ d(df)=0. }

proof.p:
Write df=f_x\,dx+f_y\,dy+f_z\,dz. Then
d(df) \amp= d(f_x)\wedge dx+d(f_y)\wedge dy+d(f_z)\wedge dz.
Expand:
d(df) \amp= (f_{xx}\,dx+f_{xy}\,dy+f_{xz}\,dz)\wedge dx \amp\quad+ (f_{yx}\,dx+f_{yy}\,dy+f_{yz}\,dz)\wedge dy \amp\quad+ (f_{zx}\,dx+f_{zy}\,dy+f_{zz}\,dz)\wedge dz.
All repeated wedges vanish. The remaining terms are
d(df) \amp= (f_{yx}-f_{xy})\,dx\wedge dy \amp\quad+ (f_{xz}-f_{zx})\,dz\wedge dx \amp\quad+ (f_{zy}-f_{yz})\,dy\wedge dz.
Since the second partial derivatives are continuous, the mixed partials agree: f_{xy}=f_{yx}, \qquad f_{xz}=f_{zx}, \qquad f_{yz}=f_{zy}. Therefore d(df)=0.

subsection.p: The smoothness condition is doing real work. It lets us trade the order of mixed partials.

statement.p: If we try to run the formula d(dg)=(g_{yx}-g_{xy})\,dx\wedge dy at the origin using these existing second partials, we get d(dg)=2\,dx\wedge dy there, not zero.
The theorem did not fail. Its hypothesis failed. The function is not C^2 at the origin, and the mixed partials are not forced to agree.

subsection.title: d(df)=0 and \nabla\times\nabla f=\mathbf 0
subsection.p: The identity d(df)=0 is the differential-form version of a classical vector identity.
This says a gradient field has no local rotation. It can push a particle uphill or downhill, but it does not create first-order spinning.

example.title: A temperature gradient has zero curl

statement.p:
Let T(x,y,z)=20+xy+yz+xz^2. Then 
abla T=(y+z^2,\ x+z,\ y+2xz). Compute the curl:

abla	imes
abla T \amp= \left(  rac{\partial}{\partial y}(y+2xz) -  rac{\partial}{\partial z}(x+z), ight. \amp\qquad  rac{\partial}{\partial z}(y+z^2) -  rac{\partial}{\partial x}(y+2xz), \amp\qquad\left.  rac{\partial}{\partial x}(x+z) -  rac{\partial}{\partial y}(y+z^2) ight) \amp= (1-1,\ 2z-2z,\ 1-1) \amp= \mathbf 0.
The cancellations are mixed partials in disguise.

subsection.title: d(d\omega)=0 and \nabla\cdot(\nabla\times\mathbf F)=0
subsection.p: Now start with a 1-form instead of a function.

Use the example from : \omega=z^2\,dx+xz\,dy+y^2\,dz. We found d\omega = (2y-x)\,dy\wedge dz + 2z\,dz\wedge dx + z\,dx\wedge dy. Now take d again:
d(d\omega) \amp= d(2y-x)\wedge dy\wedge dz + d(2z)\wedge dz\wedge dx + d(z)\wedge dx\wedge dy.
Compute: d(2y-x)=2\,dy-dx, \qquad d(2z)=2\,dz, \qquad d(z)=dz. So
d(d\omega) \amp= (2\,dy-dx)\wedge dy\wedge dz + 2\,dz\wedge dz\wedge dx + dz\wedge dx\wedge dy.
Repeated wedges vanish: dy\wedge dy=0, \qquad dz\wedge dz=0. Therefore
d(d\omega) \amp= -dx\wedge dy\wedge dz + dz\wedge dx\wedge dy.
But dz\wedge dx\wedge dy=dx\wedge dy\wedge dz, so \boxed{ d(d\omega)=0. }
The general identity is the 1-form case of d^2=0.

statement.p: Let \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz have C^2 coefficient functions on an open region. Then \boxed{ d(d\omega)=0. }

proof.p:
From , d\omega = (R_y-Q_z)\,dy\wedge dz + (P_z-R_x)\,dz\wedge dx + (Q_x-P_y)\,dx\wedge dy. Now apply d to this 2-form:
d(d\omega) \amp= \Bigl((R_y-Q_z)_x+(P_z-R_x)_y+(Q_x-P_y)_z\Bigr) \,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz.
The coefficient is R_{yx}-Q_{zx}+P_{zy}-R_{xy}+Q_{xz}-P_{yz}. Group the matching mixed partials: (R_{yx}-R_{xy}) + (P_{zy}-P_{yz}) + (Q_{xz}-Q_{zx}). Since the coefficient functions are C^2, each pair cancels. Hence d(d\omega)=0.

subsection.p: A curl field has no source strength. It can circulate, but it cannot be the whole story of a source.

statement.p: Let \mathbf F(x,y,z)=(0,xz,0). Then \nabla\times\mathbf F=(-x,0,z). Its divergence is \nabla\cdot(-x,0,z) = -1+0+1 = 0. The two surviving rates cancel. This cancellation is d^2=0 written in vector language.

subsection.p:
There is one last degree to check. If \Theta=H\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz is a 3-form in ordinary space, then
d\Theta \amp= dH\wedge dx\wedge dy\wedge dz \amp= (H_x\,dx+H_y\,dy+H_z\,dz)\wedge dx\wedge dy\wedge dz.
Every term repeats one of dx,\qquad dy,\qquad dz. So \boxed{ d\Theta=0. } In three-dimensional space, there is no nonzero 4-form waiting after a 3-form.

subsection.title: Boundary of a boundary is zero
subsection.p: Start in one dimension. For an interval I=[a,b], the oriented boundary is \partial I=\{b\}-\{a\}. A point has no boundary, so \partial(\partial I)=0.

Now look at a rectangle with vertices A,B,C,D, oriented counterclockwise: \partial R=[A,B]+[B,C]+[C,D]+[D,A]. Take the boundary of each edge:
\partial(\partial R) \amp= (B-A)+(C-B)+(D-C)+(A-D) \amp= 0.
Every vertex appears once with a plus sign and once with a minus sign.

figure.caption: The boundary of a rectangle is a closed curve. Its boundary cancels at the corners.

subsection.p: For a solid box, the same cancellation happens one dimension higher. The boundary of the box is made of faces. The boundary of those faces is made of edges. Every edge belongs to two neighboring faces, and the two induced orientations are opposite. So all edges cancel.

This explains why d^2=0 belongs with Stokes-type theorems. The general pattern is \int_{\partial M}\alpha=\int_M d\alpha. Apply the same idea one more time: \int_{\partial(\partial M)}\alpha = \int_{\partial M}d\alpha = \int_M d(d\alpha). The left side is zero because \partial(\partial M)=0. The right side is zero because d(d\alpha)=0.

That is a small sentence with a large reach. It says every exact form has zero exterior derivative: \alpha=d\beta \quad\Longrightarrow\quad d\alpha=0. But it does not answer the reverse question. If a form has zero exterior derivative, must it come from another form? On a region with no holes, often yes. Around a missing point or a missing axis, sometimes no. The next names are short, but they open the door to the topology hiding inside vector calculus: closed and exact.
